sentences.text: My name is George Scott.
It was George-- or Gyorgy-- Spiegel.
In Hungarian, it's Spiegel-- George Laszlo-- Laszly-- Leslie is my middle name.
I was born in Budapest on the 25th of May, 1930.
I am still 64, barely.
Can you go on and take us back to Budapest when you were a child and tell us what it was like growing up?
That's a long time ago.
Yet I remember things vividly.
Even when I was very young, I remember walking along my mother's side-- excuse me-- whining and crying nonstop.
And she picked me up, and she was exasperated with me.
I was less than two.
My father died when I was a year old.
And I, unfortunately, had never known him.
And-- Can you tell us your mother's and father's name please?
My father's name was Emil Spiegel, Emil.
He was a-- a soldier in the First World War and collected some sort of sickness while in prison in Russia, and unfortunately, died from it around 1931.
I was born in 1930.
My mother and father got married in Budapest in 1923.
And I wasn't born for seven years.
And I'm awfully happy that I did make it.
My mother's name was Sara, She was the third sister of a group of six sisters born to my grandfather and grandmother, her mother and father, by the name of Reich, Mondi and Ilona.
And my grandmother's maiden name was Wohl, W-O-H-L, W-O-H-L.
[SIGHS] I remember she loved to sing.
She loved to be happy.
She was, I think, affectionate when she was well.
And unfortunately, she couldn't do it on her own in the big city after she lost her husband, my father.
And she went back in 1932 to a little village called Szucsi.
It's about 60, 80 kilometers from Budapest, where my grandparents owned a farm.
They had, I think, 300 acres of vineyards and 200 acres of land of sorts, and raised, I think, four of their six daughters in this village.
My mother was born in 1900.
And my mother, from stories I gather, was a very sensitive girl, and she didn't get along too well with her father, who was super religious.
And my mother, I remember, read Oscar Wilde and other authors.
And in this time in Hungary, that wasn't the normal thing to do.
And I remember her sisters mocking her, saying that she considered herself too good to live on the farm.
And these sort of thoughts come to me much, much later on reflection.
At the time I, of course, didn't understand anything like that.
All I knew was that I missed her affection very much.
And she too, unfortunately, leaves a large gap in my own life in terms of having had a mother.
We went to-- to live with my grandparents.
I remember crawling on the floor of the kitchen.
I remember, strangely, the underside of things.
I remember how-- how big people's feet were, and how when I looked up their behind was there, and way up there was up the person.
I was a person who seemed to have absorbed everything like a blotting paper.
And even though I didn't know what to do with the information, it was there.
And I spent a lot of time by myself.
My grandfather was the father figure in my life.
And he was super religious and a nervous man who had to think of money all the time, paying the people who worked the farm, and then having dowries.
Two of the daughters-- my mother was one of them-- lost their husbands.
One, Aunt Margo, managed to remarry.

But my mother didn't, and she got sick, and was put to a hospital in the place called [NON-ENGLISH] around 1937, at which point I was in grade one, or grade two of the public school that the village had.
I was the only Jew in this school.
Oh, yes.
My teacher's name was Rosie.
And I always felt strange.
All my life I felt strange being a child.
I think primarily because other kids had moms and dads, and I didn't.
And I thought that that was a terrible injustice.
And even at school, I was kind of sitting by myself, taking things in.
I don't-- I had one good friend, who we are still friends.
His name is John Maksa, M-A-K-S-A.
He lives now in the next village.
In those days, used to be called [NON-ENGLISH].
Now it's called [NON-ENGLISH] Saint Martin.
It's about 4 kilometers to-- from the village where we grew up.
Their house was the other side of the road from me, and we used to play horses and wagons.
And we used to hide in the hay.
And it was great.
I used to lose myself with other kids, and that was-- that was a real delight.
And-- and I used to look for strange birds' eggs, and I used to love to climb down into wells.
And when I got found out, my grandmother got mortified and covered the well with a great big plank and a stone on top.
It was an exciting, mysterious time to have been at school, and even preschool.
A lot of time to myself.
I used to climb on the roof of our house.
There was a chestnut tree in front of it, and one of the branches reached over.
And I could then be unobserved and listen to all the people, all the grown-ups talking.
And I didn't understand it, but it was a fascinating experience.
Sometimes I heard them looking for me, but I didn't say anything.
There was no sound out of me.
I finished the four grades in Szucsi.
And then my grandparents-- there were no further school there, as I said, I was the only Jew in the school-- put me in a Jewish orphanage in Budapest.
It was-- it was-- excuse me-- very uncomfortable.
I-- part of this was my mother's overriding angst about being Jewish.
And she was reading various reports.
Kafka was still writing in Czechoslovakia.
Again, this comes to me on reflection.
At the time, I didn't know.
But there was a high degree of angst.
And eventually, she had to be hospitalized, where she stayed until the Germans took her out of the hospital into the ghettos in 1944.
Anyhow, in 1939, I was put into the Jewish orphanage.
And that was a terrible shock to me at first.
To begin with, I didn't know that I had-- I spoke Hungarian with the dialect of the village.
And kids can be very cruel.
I used to say [HUNGARIAN].
And of course, they laughed at me because proper Hungarian is [HUNGARIAN].
And I was very miserable.
And-- but eventually, eventually I-- I came over it.
I should say that my grandparents had a Torah in our own house, and we used to have prayer services four times a year from the neighboring villages.
Jews came to form a minyan.
And I remember hiding under my grandfather's white prayer shirt.
And everybody looks so fierce.
My grandfather had big red moustache, and it was twirled.
He looked fierce.
And occasionally he winked at me during the prayer.
Otherwise, the whole thing was almost surreal.
Then there was a wonderful cat, I remember, in the-- we had a cat, a little red cat.
I used to torture him, for which now I apologize.
But-- it-- and the roosters made wonderful noises.
It was a-- it was a village life.
In the orphanage, I-- I always had an ambivalent feeling about God.
I was overridden by the notion of sin and having said God's name in vain, et cetera.
And in the orphanage-- it must've been the first few months, there was a synagogue in the orphanage-- I remember walking into the synagogue.
There was nobody there.
And I used to be afraid of the synagogue, and I told God that that was not a fair deal that I got.
And I was not going to be afraid any longer.
And I can't tell you what a huge relief that was, to have done this.
And eventually I made friends in the orphanage, and eventually I became myself a big boy, and other little kids looked up to me.
And it was like a family, like a huge, wonderful, extended family.
And I still keep in touch with some of them who live in Israel and other places.
Well, my grandparents-- I used to, in 19-- 1940, 1941 and '42, I did summer holidays I spent on the farm.
And by then, they took away the farm from them, because they were Jews.
But they opted to-- to remain in the building, so they had one room of their house where they lived in.
And in 1943, I remember, I no longer went there. '43 was my bar mitzvah year.
And they didn't come to my bar mitzvah.
My grandfather must've been about 70 then, and my grandmother about 67.
But I do love them.
And especially my-- excuse me-- my grandmother was a very courageous lady.
There are a lot of pictures and slides that kind of envelope me.
I remember her lighting candles Friday nights, how she was crying, and I didn't understand.
She used to go and visit rabbis, and famous rabbis somewhere in eastern Hungary or Poland.
And they used to tell her what to do, and how to write things down on a paper and put it on some grave.
It all seemed mysterious to me then.
And at this point, I just think, quaint.
But there are so many riddles when a person is a child.
And I think more or less all of us start that way.
I visited my mother in the hospital once.
And I recall the sisters showed her from behind a window.
And she looked at me, and she was very thin.
And she said to the nurse, to the sister, see?
It's my big boy.
And I just looked at her, and I had a deep pain in my heart.
And I-- I couldn't do anything.
It's a feeling, you must have had to live it to know what it is.
And I don't wish it on anyone.
But in a way, it had left a lot of stuff inside me built up, which somehow or other had to be resolved.
And I attribute my way of looking at the world to the kind of very early challenges that I had to deal with and-- and get beyond.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Many of my dreams were running and being afraid, and especially being afraid of God, and knowing that I had done the wrong thing.
I had said, God, God, God, many times, and he's going to strike me dead.
And-- and unfortunately, there was no one I could talk about this.
When I was-- when I was four years old, the shochet who came twice a week to kill the animals, that they would be kosher, started teaching me Hebrew.
And of course, that was bizarre.
I had to wear my tzitzits, and that was uncomfortable because it used to slide into places where it didn't feel right.
And-- and I was terrified from my grandfather.
My grandfather, on the other hand, had six daughters.
He desperately wanted a son.
And he-- they tried often enough.
So I was the first grandson.
You can imagine the weight of all his zeal fell on me.
But at the time, I didn't understand that.
It was a tight situation, and I willingly escaped it as often as I could to my aunt Rosi, who lived in [NON-ENGLISH], about seven kilometers from the village where my grandparents lived.
And there, I was much more free, and they had a-- she and her husband, Uncle Imre Frank, had a-- a hardware store, but they also sold candy.
And there I-- it was a different world.
There were-- I didn't have to wear my tzitzits.
And-- and I made friends in that village, too, with another boy.
And they were good times.
I still think of them fondly.
Excuse me.
Yes.

The orphanage was a Jewish institution, it was-- I guess the [NON-ENGLISH], which means the Foundation of the Israelites of Budapest, or the Jewish Orphanage-- the Founded Jewish Orphanage of the Israelites of Budapest.
And some very famous people, incidentally, had grown up in this place.
I think it was in-- it was in a-- functioning since the very early 1900s.
So when I got there, it had practically 40 years of operation, or perhaps even more.
And in the morning, we had to get washed, dressed.
We had to go to prayer.
The children were from the age of five to about 20.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were four public schools in the orphanage itself, where other kids came in, not just the orphans from the area.
I missed that.
Then there was the middle school kids, and I belonged to these groups.
And we were grouped-- we were in four groups.

The little ones, who were up to grade four, the middle kids, who were up to, say, grade eight, and then the people who were trained for some sort of trade, and very few who were allowed to go to higher education-- who were very well-- who distinguished themselves as good students.
And there were half a dozen of those.
And we had a room to ourselves, the various age groups.
And the various age groups had different teachers.

There was [NON-ENGLISH], who was looking after the little ones, who incidentally is living in Toronto-- married one of the older boys, who became a-- a radiologist, who just retired, by the name of Pater-- Dr.
Pater.
And there was my group, and I had a teacher called Mr.
Wolfe, who was only eight years older than I.
But it seemed to me like, at the time, he was much older.
And there was another group, who was looked after by Mr.
Leibowitz.
The director's name was Mr.
Lederer.
He was a wonderful person.
One day I was walking in the basement of the orphanage-- and I was practically born this way, afraid of my own shadow-- and suddenly I see the director, and I just wanted to disappear.
And I am walking along, and I suddenly feel his arm on my shoulder, and he planted a big kiss on my face.
And then he kept walking.
As long as I live, I'll never forget this.
You know, I still-- I'm on the verge of crying just to remember it, how-- how wonderful it is to be-- to be courageous enough to show affection.
Because I think that's what everyone needs, really, whether we admit it or not.
So-- and there was the sewing place where our clothes were issued.
There was a lady called Mrs.
Pfeiffer, who was a cripple, but a very feisty person.
And she was always critical.
We were kneeling too much, or we were sliding on our behinds, or we weren't taking care of our clothes.
We had two sets of clothes-- one to wear in the building, one in-- uniforms both-- one in which we marched to school.
And you could see, we were in the [NON-ENGLISH] Budapest-- a very elegant area, close to the Liget.
You could see four, five, six kids marching, all in black uniform with black caps in the morning, going to school, and at noon.
Coming back from school.
It had an ethos all of its own.
And-- and-- it was-- you know, I really learned to love this place.
I became friendly with a boy my age called Steven Proper.
And his father was a-- a scout master, and they were on some sort of outings, and the boat turned over, and the kids fell in, and the father jumped in to save them-- and they all got drowned.
Including the father.
So he came in the orphanage.
And-- and six, seven months, we were in the same class.
We became very close friends.
He was a-- if possible, he was even more shy than I was.
Quiet man.
And that's-- unfortunately, I found out some years back that he had a brain tumor, and he committed suicide.
But I do have a picture of him in my album.
He left a little girl who I was unable to trace.
Anti-Semitism was always there, like a cloud.
It was a terrible feeling.
I never, ever felt like a Jew.
I still don't feel like a Jew.
I feel like me.
I-- I-- I naturally, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] think of myself, and feel that I belong to the Jewish people.
But I-- I-- I don't know if it's a fair classification.
And who said that fear has ever been an issue of-- of trying to group people?
I often make the analogy of talking about people with size eight shoes, or with blue eyes.
It's-- it's-- it's been terribly unfair.
And when I think of what this fear did to my own mother, you know, it's useless for me to speculate how it could have been, had she been well and strong.
But she wasn't well, and she wasn't strong.
And really, this is something that had never, ever been-- in my purview, it was out of my own hands.
I remember my grandfather used to go to a place called [NON-ENGLISH], the place where my mother was eventually hospitalized.
Not too often, maybe five times a year.
And he used to come back with little red hats for me.
And that was the-- what the young Hungarian soldiers wore.
I remember at school, the-- in fact the sons of the teacher who was teaching me, kept shouting at me, shouting, Jewish [HUNGARIAN]-- which means, look, what a joke, a Jewish soldier.
And I-- I just-- I just ran.
It seems to me so much of my life-- and I find that most regrettable-- had been lived in flight.
And I do notice various kinds of flight patterns right now, right here.
You don't have to be Jewish to know what I'm talking about.
And anti-Semitism, of course, was more than registered for me as-- as my life unfolded, albeit that it is a happy kind of finish, for which I am profoundly grateful.
Well, in the orphanage, we heard stories.
We heard people running away from Czechoslovakia, from Poland.
And we had some kids in the orphanage-- smf we were so cruel, because these kids didn't speak Hungarian, they spoke Yiddish.
And they used to say [YIDDISH]-- and-- which means where are you from?
So we used to call them 'Finns' among ourselves-- which is an awful thing to do.
So just as kids had been cruel with me, when I spoke Hungarian with a funny dialect, I didn't give a second thought to do this to some other kids who were really had enough on their plates.
As a matter of fact, just three months ago, I met one of them in Israel in Ramot Naftali, whose wife is coming in a couple of months.
She's the head of [NON-ENGLISH] in northern Galilee.
And he remembered how-- how cruel Hungarian Jews were to him, who spoke no English.
He came to the orphanage.
But I didn't recall him-- not until he showed me his wedding picture.
And then I remembered him vaguely.
But he was not in the group I was in.
Of course, they took away the land of my grandparents by degrees.
And no one-- no one could be unaware of anti-Semitism.
In the orphanage, we were relatively sheltered.
The director, Mr.
Lederer, who I spoke to you about, he came because in 1940, he was kicked out from the German school where he taught philosophy for 20 years.
And he was very appreciative of the orderly Germanic approach to things.
And after the war, in some ways, that was his undoing, which I most regret because some of the boys got him into trouble.
Not about being German, but about being anti-communist.
And he was religious.
And that was a taboo, once the communists took hold.
But by 1947, by the end of 1947, I wasn't in the orphanage.
You know, after the camp, I came back to the orphanage.
So there were many signs.
The Germans came into-- sorry for interrupting you-- Germans came into-- to Hungary in March of 1943.
And-- and within about a month, the law came out that all Jews had to wear a yellow star.
And there was a boy in the orphanage who ran away from Czechoslovakia a year or so earlier, by the name of Farkash.
And he said that, let's go back to Czechoslovakia because the Germans killed all the Jews there.
We'd be safe there.
We'd join the partisans, and we'd fight.
And I was just turned 14, and I thought, gee, what a great idea.
So we didn't put our stars on.
We snuck out of the orphanage.
We said nothing to no one.
We got on the train.
We marched to-- we got off at this border village.
We started walking toward the border.
And we saw a building in the distance.
And as we got closer, we saw some soldiers in the building, in front of the building.
And we knew that the soldiers had seen us, so we just kept coming.
And when we got there, they asked for our papers.
Of course, we had no papers.
And they asked us to please wait.
And in about 20 minutes, a car came and took us back to the village, and they locked us up in a house.
And in this house, on a window sill, I saw a stack of [NON-ENGLISH].
And there was this constriction in my throat.
I cannot forget.
And Farkash said nothing.
We spoke very little.
And we seem to have been kind of absorbed in-- in the depth of our own infinities.
No, we didn't.
No, we didn't.
And if-- if-- if people talked about it, we didn't want to know.
It was so unthinkable.
We liked to believe that people were taken to labor camps, where they would have jobs, and we would work.
No one had anything against working.
And we knew that the war was really coming to an end.
And we just hoped that we would survive.
